questions.text: Complete the sentences by choosing the most appropriate option, from the given lettered choices (A to D/E) below:He would like _ have a job in the same office as his brother.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: “Too” is an adverb of degree that can mean “excessively,” “also,” or “very.” It is used before an adjective or adverb or before “much”, “many”, “few” etc. It is used when the speaker wants to show so much of a particular quality that something is not possible (Example: I was too excited to sleep.) or when the speaker wants to express that something is more than necessary or acceptable (Example: You’re driving too fast.). It is also used after mentioning an additional person, thing, or fact to show that they are also included in what you are saying (Example: Sana’s got a lovely voice and she’s a good dancer too.).
“To” is a preposition. It can be used to indicate a goal or a direction of movement, as well as a place of arrival. Thus, choosing “to” to complete the given sentence would be most appropriate.

“Of” and “to” are two separate prepositions that can’t be used as a prepositional phrase because it only possible to use two prepositions together if the second one is creating a noun phrase. Hence, “of to” does not make sense grammatically.

The verb “get” is an irregular verb. Its three parts are get, got, gotten. It is used as get + object or get + indirect object + direct object when the speaker wants to express the meaning of “fetch” or “receive” or “obtain” (Example: Can you get me a glass of water?). It is also used as a get + adjective to describe a change of state or situation (Example: Let’s hope she gets better soon.).

questions.explanation: The correct preposition to use over here will be "to" because all others don't make sense here grammatically. Too is used to imply collectiveness, which is not the context here. Of to and get don't make any sense when plugged in.

questions.text: It is natural for us to exert _ our own success
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: incorrect because "in" is not the appropriate pronoun for exert.
"exert at" is the most appropriate choice.
This does not match the question.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Exert means to apply force. And in this question, it is natural for us to exert AT our own success.

questions.text: Saba was sick on the bus.[The underlined prepositional phrase functions as a _ in this sentence:]
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Adjuncts are regular adverbs of time, place, manner, degree, and frequency. They are essential to the meaning of the sentence, however, the phrase, “on the bus” has no clear impact on the sentence and the sentence won’t lose its meaning if we drop the phrase.
This is incorrect. Disjunct expresses the writer’s opinion like “sadly”, “realistically” etc.
This option is incorrect. Conjuncts link two clauses or sentences such as “however” etc.
Adverbials give more information about verbs and even if dropped, it won’t have a significant impact on the sentence hence D is the right answer.

questions.explanation: Adverbials give more information about verbs and even if dropped, it won’t have a significant impact on the sentence hence D is the right answer.

questions.text: Father must _ left his briefcase at the office.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Of" is a preposition, and it doesn't fit into the blank in the given sentence. Prepositions are used to show relationships between nouns or pronouns and other words in a sentence. In this context, "of" does not make sense and is not the correct option.

"Had" is the past participle form of the verb "have." In this sentence, it could be used in the past perfect tense to indicate an action that was completed before another past action. However, when combined with "must" in the sentence, it creates redundancy and doesn't make grammatical sense. So, "had" is not the correct option here.

"Have" is the base form of the verb, and it can be used in the sentence to create the phrase "must have." This construction is used for speculating about a past action that is likely to have happened. So, the correct option is "Option C: Have." The complete sentence is: "Father must have left his briefcase at the office."
"Opt" is a verb that means to choose or select. However, it does not fit the context of the sentence, and it is not the correct option.

questions.explanation: We use must have + past participle when we are sure of what happened. Hence "must have" is correct. ‘Of’ is used to show ownership or a distance from something or a result coming from something. As the sentence is not in the third person singular, ‘has’ is not used. ‘Are’ is used for plural nouns, which is not the case in this sentence ('Father' is singular). ‘Opt’ is used to make a choice, especially of one thing or possibility, instead of others. In this case, the briefcase is an essential item and is necessary.

questions.text: Demonstrate correct use of articles and prepositionsMy friend has been living _ Karachi for two years.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The preposition "on" is used to indicate a surface or a position that is physically above or attached to something.
The preposition "at" is used to indicate a specific location or point in time, but it does not convey the meaning of living in a place.
The preposition "in" is used to indicate the location of a place where someone lives, example: Karachi
The preposition "across" is used to indicate movement from one side of something to the other

questions.explanation: "In" is a preposition used when something is enclosed or located within a defined space, e.g., Karachi. The preposition "on" is used to indicate a surface or a position that is physically above or attached to something.The preposition "at" is used to indicate a specific location or point in time, but it does not convey the meaning of living in a place.The preposition "in" is used to indicate the location of a place where someone lives, for example, Karachi The preposition "across" is used to indicate movement from one side of something to the other.

questions.text: I'll show you a picture _ the palace.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Off means closing something.
Of is used to show any place or thing.
"In" is used for inside.
"At" is used for showing one specific place.

questions.explanation: Of is used to show any place or thing.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the correct option:You should stick _ your promise.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "You should stick by your promise" is not the correct option because the phrase "stick by" means to support or remain loyal to someone, rather than staying true to a promise, but it has a slightly different meaning. It means to support or stand by someone, rather than keeping a promise. For example, "I will stick by you no matter what."
"You should stick to your promise" is the correct option. This phrase means to remain committed to or follow through on a promise.

"You should stick with your promise" is not the correct option because the phrase "stick with" is typically used to mean staying loyal to someone or something, rather than specifically referring to keeping a promise. For example, "I will stick with this company even during tough times."

"You should stick in your promise" is not the correct option because the phrase "stick in" is not an idiomatic expression and does not convey the intended meaning of keeping a promise. It does not fit in the context of the sentence.

questions.explanation: "You should stick to your promise" is the correct option. This phrase means to remain committed to or follow through on a promise.

questions.text: Fill in with appropriate the Preposition: We saw a cricket match _school stadium.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: We saw a cricket match over school stadium is the incorrect sentence.
We saw a cricket match of school stadium, which is the incorrect sentence.
We saw a cricket match at school stadium is the correct sentence.
We saw a cricket match on school stadium, which is the incorrect sentence.

questions.explanation: We saw a cricket match at school stadium is the correct sentence.

questions.text: Complete the sentences by choosing the most appropriate option, from the given lettered choices. Two airplanes crashed _ the fog yesterday?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "On" would imply that the airplanes crashed on top of the fog, which doesn't make sense in this context.
"Of" suggests a possession or belonging relationship, which is not relevant to describe the crash in relation to the fog.
When we say that two airplanes crashed "in" the fog, we are describing the location or environment where the crash occurred.
This doesn't make sense in the context

questions.explanation: The most appropriate choice to complete the sentence is "in." When we say that two airplanes crashed "in" the fog, we are describing the location or environment where the crash occurred. In this case, the fog is acting as a descriptive element, indicating that the crash took place within the foggy conditions. Using "on" or "of" would not convey the same meaning. "On" would imply that the airplanes crashed on top of the fog, which doesn't make sense in this context. "Of" suggests a possession or belonging relationship, which is not relevant to describe the crash in relation to the fog. Therefore, "in" is the most suitable choice in this sentence.

questions.text: Select the correct option:He was killed _ a robber _ a hatchet.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The preposition ”from” typically indicates a point of origin its source, rather than an agent of an action. Additionally “with” is used to describe the instrument or means, in this case the instrument is the hatchet, not the robber.
The preposition “at” typically indicates a location or place but “by” is the correct preposition.
The preposition “through” typically indicates a movement or a process of passing from one side to another. Similarly, “far” indicates distance.
In the first blank, we will use "BY" to indicate a preposition showing accountability, while in the second blank we will use a preposition "WITH" to indicate Possession or weapon of action.

questions.explanation: The first blank needs a preposition, that shows property and accountability, hence ‘by’ is the best choice here. The second blank needs a word that means 'to refer to something', hence 'with' will be the best option here.

questions.text: Complete the sentences by choosing the most appropriate option, from the given lettered choices (A to D) below each:Her hair was hanging _ her back.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The verb “paint” means to cover the surface (of something) with paint (noun). It is used as the action verb when the subject is covering the surface (of something) with paint in the present.

The verb “painted” is the past tense and the past particle of the verb “paint”. It is used as the action when the subject covered the surface (of something) with paint in the past, an action that is finished and completed in active voice.
The verb phrase “has painted” is used in present perfect tense, for an action that the subject started at some time in the past and is still continuing now.

The verb phrase “was painted” is the past simple passive. Passive voice is used to describe past events, focusing on the object of a verb to emphasize who or what receives an action instead of the subject (the person or thing) that did the action who/which is unknown, unimportant or not the focus of our interest. The verb phrase “was painted” is used to describe that the object got covered with paint. Thus, choosing “was painted” to complete the given sentence would be the most appropriate.

questions.explanation: The key to solving these questions is reading them in your head, filling in each option and then deciphering which option fits in well. For any sentence which refers to something hanging, down is the most suitable preposition.

questions.text: Choose the BEST Option.The region _ which they were passing was known as the Land of Thirst and Death.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Through can be used as a preposition, an adverb, and an adjective. It has several meanings, including "from one side to the other," "from beginning to end," and "during an entire period": He rode his bicycle through the door and onto the street. You have to read the book through if you want to be prepared for class.
By means ‘as a means of’ something or someone. This preposition often comes in a passive sentence.Example:Do you travel by bus/train/plane/bike? He was undone by me. The letter was written by him.

In (to indicate a location)- I am in my friend’s place now.In (used while doing something) — The tagline should be catchy in marketing a product.In (to indicate opinion, belief, feeling, etc.)- I believe in hardwork.In (specify day, month, season, year) — I prefer to do Maths in the morning.The new academic session will commence in March.In (to indicate colour, shape and size) — This dress comes in four sizes.
'From' is used to show where somebody/something starts, what the origin of somebody/something is, how far apart two places are, somebody’s position or point of view etc

questions.explanation: Through can be used as a preposition, an adverb, and an adjective. It has several meanings, including “from one side to the other,” “from beginning to end,” and “during an entire period”: He rode his bicycle through the door and onto the street. You have to read the book through if you want to be prepared for class.

questions.text: Although he studied hard, he couldn't succeed _ getting a high score in his test.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "In" is used in space and in this case the test.
This would suggest he couldn't succeed "on" the test itself, which doesn't quite fit the context.
This would imply he couldn't succeed "at" the overall goal of getting a high score, rather than the process of achieving it.
This doesn't properly complete the sentence, as "to getting a high score" doesn't convey the right meaning.

questions.explanation: “In” is used in space and in this case the test. "On" would be used to indicate position. "At" would indicate a specific time or place, and "to" would indicate a goal or direction of movement.

questions.text: He got up early _ 8 o'clock _ the morning. Fill the spaces with the most appropriate words.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Incorrect because "on" is used for days and dates, not specific times.
Correct because "at" is used for specific times, and "in" is used for parts of the day.
Incorrect because "on" is not used for parts of the day.
Incorrect because "upon" is not typically used to indicate parts of the day in this context.

questions.explanation: Usually, the preposition that is used to refer to time is ''at''. In some cases, ''by'' may also be used. Additionally, when we do not specify a fixed time and refer to a time period such as morning, evening, afternoon, etc. then we use ''in". Hence, ''He got up early at 8 o'clock in the morning" is the only correct answer here.

questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Incorrect because "confided about" is not a correct construction.
Correct because "confided in" means to trust someone with private information.
Incorrect because "confided on" is not a correct construction.
Incorrect because "confided of" is not a correct construction.

questions.explanation: The phrasal verb "confide" usually goes with the preposition "in", and it is actually an idiom

questions.text: The plane will stay on the runway _ five minutes.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: We use for to talk about a purpose or a reason for something.
"In" is incorrect because it doesn't convey the intended meaning of the plane's duration on the runway.
"Below" is incorrect as it is not applicable to expressing a timeframe for staying on the runway.
"On" is incorrect because it doesn't indicate a specific duration; it simply mentions the plane being on the runway without specifying the time.

questions.explanation: The plane will stay on the runway for five minutes.

questions.text: The Police do not understand how the thieves _ the house.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Got into: the house is the correct answer. The sentence involves a phrasal verb ‘got into’ which means enter into the house.
Got in: While grammatically correct, it doesn't fit the sentence structure as it lacks the preposition "into."
Get into: This is the base form of the verb, but the sentence requires the past tense.
Get in: This is the base form of the verb and doesn't convey the past tense required in the sentence.

questions.explanation: ‘Got into’ the house is the correct answer. The sentence involves a phrasal verb ‘got into’ which means enter into the house.

questions.text: He placed the book _ the vase on the table.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Correct because "beside" means next to or alongside.
Incorrect because "after" suggests a sequence in time, not a physical location.
Incorrect because "on" would imply the book is on top of the vase, which doesn't fit the context.
Incorrect because "in" suggests placing something inside another object, which doesn't fit the context.

questions.explanation: The correct preposition in this sentence is beside, which means next to something.

questions.text: I woke up _ five o'clock _ the morning.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: a) I woke up for five o'clock in the morning: This option is incorrect. "For" is not the correct preposition to use in this context. We usually use "at" instead of "for" when indicating a specific time.
b) I woke up at five o'clock in the morning: This option is correct. "At" is the correct preposition to use when specifying a particular time.
c) I woke up in five o'clock at the morning: This option is incorrect. "In" is not the correct preposition to use here. Additionally, we use "morning" without the article "the."
d) I woke up at five o'clock for the morning: This option is incorrect. Again, "for" is not the correct preposition to use in this context.

questions.explanation: “At” and “in” are the correct prepositions. The preposition “for” is used to refer to an extended period; a duration (how long something lasts). The preposition “in” is used to refer to periods of time during the day (not specific times), such as the morning. The preposition “at” is used to refer to specific times on the clock or points of time in the day; a precise time, such as five o’clock.
